summary:
Mycenaean civilization dominated the Greek mainland and Aegean from approximately 1600 to 1100 BC, representing the first advanced civilization in Europe and the cultural backdrop for the earliest Greek literary traditions. Centered on fortified palace complexes at Mycenae, Tiryns, Pylos, and Thebes, the Mycenaeans were a warrior aristocracy whose kings accumulated vast wealth through conquest, trade, and tribute.

Mycenaean palaces functioned as administrative and redistributive centers, managing agricultural surpluses, craft production, and military resources through a bureaucratic system recorded in [[event:1823|Linear B]], an early form of Greek adapted from Minoan Linear A. Linear B tablets found at Pylos and Knossos provide detailed inventories of personnel, livestock, land holdings, and stored commodities, revealing a highly organized palace economy. The Mycenaeans absorbed significant cultural influences from the [[event:53|Minoan civilization]], including artistic styles, religious symbols, and writing.

The [[event:1895|Shaft Graves of Mycenae]], dating to around 1600–1500 BC, contained extraordinary wealth — gold masks, inlaid daggers, amber jewelry, and bronze weapons — demonstrating the sudden rise of a militaristic ruling class. Later monumental construction included the impressive [[event:1896|Lion Gate of Mycenae]] and the cyclopean stone walls that surrounded major citadels. Mycenaean traders and warriors ranged widely across the eastern Mediterranean, establishing connections with Egypt, the Levant, and Anatolia.

The Mycenaean world collapsed around 1100 BC in conjunction with the wider [[event:1815|Bronze Age Collapse]], with palace centers burned, populations displaced, and the Linear B writing system lost. The [[event:1826|Greek Dark Ages]] that followed saw a dramatic decline in population, trade, and material culture. Despite this rupture, Mycenaean memory was preserved in oral tradition, eventually forming the kernel of the Homeric epics and the mythological universe that defined classical Greek culture.
